serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g (one cup or glass), as typically served in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls and casual dining restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Mango, raw (USDA FoodData Central ID 169910) and Basil seeds / sabja seeds nutrient profile (USDA SR Legacy); composite estimation for mango-basil-seed drink
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for mango basil seeds drink in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values estimated by component analysis: approximately 60% mango pulp juice (USDA FDC 169910), 5% hydrated basil seeds (USDA SR Legacy sabja/chia analogue), 30% water, and 5% added sugar. Per-100g values reflect a diluted sweetened mango drink with basil seeds. Energy and macros cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA fruit beverage category data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mango (Mangifera indica) and fruit-based drinks; Atkinson FS et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for mango basil seeds drink. GI of 51 estimated from mango juice / mango drink analogues reported in the University of Sydney GI Database (mango GI range 41–60, central estimate ~51 for sweetened mango drinks). Basil seeds contribute dietary fibre which may modestly lower postprandial glucose response. GL per serving = (51 × 12.5 × 300/100) / 100 = 19.1 g. GI category: low (≤55).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~15 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: With only approximately 15 mg sodium per serving, this drink poses minimal sodium load for hypertensive individuals; however, portion control is advised due to natural fruit sugars that may contribute to weight gain, an indirect risk factor for hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~19 g per serving) driven by mango sugars and added sweetener
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 19 g per 300 g serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, request reduced or no added sugar, and note that basil seeds provide soluble fibre which may attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and cholesterol; moderate sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This drink is very low in saturated fat (0.04 g/100g) and cholesterol (0 mg/100g), making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet, though individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor total sugar intake and prefer unsweetened or lightly sweetened versions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango with Basil Seeds Drink (also known as Mango Basil Seeds or Mango Sabja Drink) is a popular chilled beverage served in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls, casual restaurants, and bubble tea shops. It consists of blended or pureed ripe mango mixed with water or coconut water, sweetened with sugar or syrup, and enriched with hydrated basil seeds — known locally as biji selasih (Malay), sabja or tukmaria (South Asian terminology). The basil seeds (Ocimum basilicum) are soaked in water until they swell into a gelatinous, tapioca-like texture, adding a pleasant chewy mouthfeel to the smooth, tropical mango base. The drink is bright orange-yellow in colour, intensely fruity and sweet with a mild floral note, and is typically served cold over ice. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~52 kcal/100g), predominantly from natural mango sugars and any added sweetener, with minimal fat and modest protein. Basil seeds contribute soluble dietary fibre, which may help moderate postprandial glucose response. The drink is a source of vitamin C (~14 mg/100g) and provitamin A (~38 mcg RAE/100g) from mango. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, and halal. Clinically, the moderate glycemic load (~19 g per standard 300 g serving) means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control and prefer reduced-sugar versions. The very low sodium and saturated fat content make it suitable for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients in moderate amounts.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mango basil seeds drink was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values were estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for raw mango (FDC 169910) and basil/chia seed analogues, adjusted for dilution with water and added sugar typical of this drink format. GI was estimated from mango drink analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and grounded in approved sources, but no exact match exists.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central (mango raw FDC 169910, basil seed analogue) and FAO/INFOODS SEA fruit beverage category; no exact database entry exists for this specific drink. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a fruit-based drink with no hydrogenated fats; naturally zero by composition. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage containing no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a vegan plant-based drink with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as mango and basil seeds contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FDC mango entry. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central mango entry or basil seed analogue data used for this estimation. 7. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate from mango drink data in the University of Sydney GI Database; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). 8. Serving size of 300 g reflects a standard cup/glass as served in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker settings. 9. The food_group has been reclassified from 'Mixed' (input) to 'Beverages' as this is a drink product. 10. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the dish is served in Singapore by a Singapore-based restaurant. 11. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from USDA mango fatty acid profile proportionally adjusted for dilution.
